summary: Hannes Mühleisen discusses DuckDB's origins, philosophy, and explosive growth as an embedded analytical database. He explains why DuckDB prioritizes user experience, zero-dependency installation, and state-of-the-art query processing in a simple package. He critiques Lakehouse formats as technically inferior recreations of data warehouse features driven by market forces rather than technical merit. He argues the industry has been solving 'whale problems' while neglecting the 99% of users whose data fits on a single node, and advocates for transactional semantics in analytics without performance penalties.
key_points: DuckDB's success comes from packaging orthodox database technology in a frictionless, dependency-free experience that prioritizes users over benchmarks
Lakehouse formats like Iceberg are rebuilding data warehouse features in an inferior way—limited to roughly one transaction per second—driven by market politics rather than technical needs
The industry spent 20 years solving 'whale problems' (Google, Netflix scale) while neglecting the 99% of real-world data workloads that fit on a single node
DuckDB implements full ACID transactional semantics for analytics with innovations like speculative WAL writing and graceful degradation to disk when memory is exceeded
The extension ecosystem is DuckDB's future direction, becoming a runtime fabric hosting a Cambrian explosion of community-built capabilities
money_quotes: What if we take the body of knowledge, the orthodoxy of what the database engine should look like and just put it into a package that doesn't make you hate everything and everyone around you?
We are essentially building things that are inferior to the state of the art from 20 years ago. And somehow get excited about it.
I don't think anybody can realistically show me a path to more than one transaction per second on an Iceberg table.
We cannot tell a boat sales company to install Spark. There's no point for them to run Spark to deal with their customer data. Yet we have been telling them that for the last 10 years.
If Oracle hadn't charged per CPU, would NoSQL ever have happened?
We took this Galaxy Quest principle to query processing. We just never want to give up. We never want to surrender.
Raw performance is overrated. You need to solve a problem. You're not solving the problem by being 2% faster and then the user not being able to install it.
I think making the user experience great is something that has not occurred to anyone in databases yet.
key_insight: The database industry wasted a decade solving problems only tech giants have, while the real opportunity was packaging proven database technology into something ordinary users could actually install and use.
